extracted_json.title: Structured Problem-Solving Process (SPSP): Steps 1 & 2

blocks.text: Step 1 of the SPSP : Definition of the problem

items.text: How attractive is an industry from the point of view of the players?
What position or competitive strategy should a competitor (in the industry) adopt?

blocks.text: Step 2 of the SPSP: Plan or Process

items.text: Create a structural map of the industry/market (I/M) landscape in which the company is embedded
Perform a five (or six) forces analysis of the I/M landscape
Determine the attractiveness (as measured by the profit potential, the Return on Invested Capital [ROIC]) of the industry
Determine or decide on the company's position or competitive strategy.
What are the relationships between the players in the I/M landscape.

extracted_json.summary_topic_line: Steps 1 and 2 of the Structured Problem-Solving Process (SPSP), including Porter's defining questions and the 5-step plan.
extracted_json.summary_gist: Notes outline the first two steps of the SPSP. Step 1 (define the problem) frames Porter's two guiding questions about industry attractiveness and the competitive strategy a competitor should adopt, with the 1980s PC industry as an example. Step 2 (plan/process) lists a 5-step procedure: map the industry/market (I/M) landscape, run a five (or six) forces analysis, assess attractiveness via profit potential/ROIC, decide the company's competitive position, and analyze relationships among players in the landscape.

transcription:
Step 1 of the SPSP : Definition of the problem
         Porter asked the following questions
              - How attractive is an industry from
                the point of view of the players ?
              - What position or competitive strategy
                should a competitor (in the industry)
                adopt ?

     Example : The Personal Computer Industry ≈ 1980s

     Step 2 of the SPSP: Plan or Process :
              5-step process
                 [figure]

   1. Create a structural map of the industry/market
      (I/M) landscape in which the company is embedded

   2. Perform a five (or six) forces analysis of the
      I/M landscape

   3. Determine the attractiveness (as measured by
      the profit potential, the Return on Invested
      Capital [ROIC]) of the industry

   4. Determine or decide on the company's
      position or competitive strategy.

   5. What are the relationships between the
      players in the I/M landscape.
